title: Performance Analysis of Underwater Quantum Key Distribution Protocols: BB84, SARG04, and BBM92
authors: Nour Rizk, Angélique Drémeau, Arnaud Coatanhay
abstract: Underwater quantum key distribution (UQKD) ensures unconditional communication security based on the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics. This study examines the performance of the BB84, SARG04, and the entanglement-based BBM92 protocols under the impact of both system optical parameters and the physical effects of the underwater channel. The main performance criteria considered are the quantum bit error rate (QBER) and the quantum correlation between the communicating parties as functions of the propagation distance in three types of non-turbulent seawater: clear, coastal, and turbid. For the BBM92 protocol, the quantum channel is modeled using Kraus operators, which characterize the combined effects of attenuation and depolarization on maximally-entangled photons, allowing the derivation of our analytical expression of the QBER, based on the correlation losses between measurements. Monte Carlo simulation results validate the analytical expression of the QBER for all the studied protocols, under various water types, atmospheric conditions, and system parameters. The results determine the conditions under which QKD protocols achieve optimal performance for secure and efficient underwater quantum communications.
summary: This paper rigorously analyzes the performance of BB84, SARG04, and BBM92 QKD protocols over simulated underwater quantum channels. It models the complex noise effects of the water using Kraus operators to calculate key metrics like QBER and quantum correlation. The findings demonstrate that environmental factors like turbidity and ambient light are critical limitations, dictating the achievable secure communication distance.
main_problem: The study analyzes the performance of three Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols (BB84, SARG04, and BBM92) when implemented over a realistic underwater quantum channel.
main_result: The maximum secure transmission distance is highly dependent on water turbidity, ambient irradiance (day vs. night), and the specific QKD protocol used, with depolarization and scattering being major limiting factors.
method: The performance is analyzed by deriving analytical expressions for the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) and Quantum Gain ($Q_\mu$), which are then validated using Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: The physical system is an underwater optical channel, modeled as a noisy open quantum system. The quantum evolution is described by a Completely Positive and Trace-Preserving (CPTP) map using Kraus operators to account for attenuation and depolarization.
key_observables: Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Quantum Correlation ($\langle\sigma_x \otimes\sigma_xangle$), and Quantum Gain ($Q_\mu$).
important_parameters: Water type (clear, coastal, turbid), ambient irradiance (daytime vs. nighttime), pupil diameter, and source position relative to Alice and Bob.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes non-turbulent channels for some derivations, and the modeling incorporates approximations for photon statistics (Poissonian) and channel noise sources (background light, dark counts).
figures_summary: Figures compare QBER vs. distance for BB84 and SARG04 across different water types and irradiance levels, and show the dependence of QBER and quantum correlation on source position for BBM92.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the three protocols, deriving QBER formulas for each, modeling the non-unitary channel evolution using Kraus operators for damping and depolarization, and validating these analytical results against extensive Monte Carlo simulations across various environmental scenarios.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed, quantitative analysis of decoherence and noise effects (depolarization, attenuation) on fundamental quantum communication protocols, which is crucial for developing practical quantum technologies in harsh environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
